metadata.title: The Eight-Field Instructional Brief
metadata.description_blurb: A topic-description prompt produces plausible prose that fits nobody. The eight-field brief turns Claude into a design partner by making instructional specification explicit before any draft is generated.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged

swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B06: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: A topic-description prompt produces plausible prose that fits nobody. Give Claude a lesson on opportunity cost and you get a lesson on opportunity cost — fluent, coherent, and calibrated for a student who does not exist in any actual classroom. The eight-field instructional brief makes Claude a design partner instead. This is Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: Give me a lesson on opportunity cost for my intro economics class

beats.narration_text: The failure is the topic prompt. Marta asked for a lesson on cells. Priya asked for a lesson on cells as dynamic systems, with the specific misconception her students hold — that cells are static containers — named as a field in the brief. Priya's lesson was usable on Monday. Marta's needed thirty minutes of editing to remove the things her students were not ready for.
beats.new_visual_element: Topic vs situation — Marta/Priya contrast, two prompts side by side

beats.narration_text: Field three is the pivotal difference: the misconception. What do students actually believe that is wrong or incomplete? Not 'students may struggle with this' — that is vague. 'Students believe photosynthesis only happens when there is direct sunlight, and most think plants get their mass from the soil' — that is a misconception. Claude can now address what is actually in the room.
beats.new_visual_element: Field 3: Misconception — what students actually believe

beats.narration_text: Field four: the outcome. Not 'understand photosynthesis.' That is untestable. 'Students will be able to explain why a plant kept in the dark loses mass over two weeks' — that is a specific, observable, falsifiable outcome. Claude can now design backward from a real test of learning, not a subject label.

The eight fields together: learning objective, prior knowledge, misconception, specific outcome, format constraints, must-not rules, review criteria, and the teacher's own non-negotiables. Fluent output aligned to nobody is the failure. Eight fields is the spec that makes alignment possible — and alignment is your review job, not Claude's.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — eight-field brief template, all fields visible

beats.narration_text: Fill the eight fields for your next lesson. Paste this — Claude will ask you for each one.

props.command: Help me fill an eight-field instructional brief for a lesson I am planning. Ask me: learning objective, prior knowledge level, the specific misconception students hold, the testable outcome I want, format constraints, must-not rules, review criteria, and my non-negotiables. Then draft the lesson.

beats.narration_text: If this makes your next lesson usable on Monday, subscribe. Liam, in for Bear.
